title: Magic Printers and Free Energy
summary: Economics: the study of the allocation of scarce resources. But what if nothing is scarce? What if, on a whim, you could print whatever you want? Probably disaster. Matter printers are a constant theme throughout science fiction [citation needed] . Imagine a 3D printer that can make anything. Yes, anything. Not just out of plastic, but fully-formed objects.…
content:
Economics: the study of the allocation of scarce resources.

But what if nothing is scarce? What if, on a whim, you could print whatever you want? Probably disaster.

Matter printers are a constant theme throughout science fiction[citation needed]. Imagine a 3D printer that can make anything. Yes, anything. Not just out of plastic, but fully-formed objects. Imagine telling a matter printer to create the perfect soufflé, and moments later have a perfectly-delicate and just-sweet dish in front of you.

But it can go one step further! A matter printer can make more matter printers, so the printers themselves can propagate throughout society pretty fast. Food scarcity? Solved. Water scarcity? Solved. Insulin scarcity? Solved. Sounds pretty good right?

A quick physics and math lesson first.

Naturally, we know that matter cannot be created nor destroyed. Neither can energy. However, we do have Einstein to help us out here. Matter has an energy value, and energy converts into matter, and vice versa. All we need is the equation E=mc2. Energy equals mass times the speed of light squared. Let's do a quick unit analysis of this.

Mass is in kilograms. The speed of light is in meters per second. Therefore, kg times (ms-1)2 evaluates to kg m2s-2, which happens to be the definition of a joule.

One kilogram of matter can therefore be converted into pure energy. The speed of light is 299,792,458 ms-1. Therefore, plugging those values into E=mc2 gives us 8.9•1015 joules. WolframAlpha also helpfully tells us that is 0.21% of the solution set of a 3x3x3 Rubik's cube.

To put this amount of energy into perspective, the world used 617,336 petajoules in 2019 according to the International Energy Agency. Converting that value into kilograms is as simple as dividing that number by the speed of light squared. WolframAlpha is back to the rescue and tells us that the world uses the equivalent of about 7,000 kilograms of energy. It sounds weird measuring energy in kilograms, doesn't it? 7,000 kilograms is about as much mass in a mid-size garbage dumpster.

So that is the scientific basis for this invention. It is possible, according to physics, to convert matter into energy, and energy back into matter; and the rest can be done by nanomachines, or whatever.

Let's also assume that the same technology is used for matter-matter conversion as well as energy production. I am the author of this piece, after all.

In The Short-Term

What happens the scant few moments after the matter printer and matter-energy converter is created and is found to work? I hope that the scientists take a week off to think about the ramifications of their invention (and hopefully read this article).

But, oh no! Who is this? Some idiot decides to steal the printer and energy converter and the blueprints for them!

And since she also read this article, the first thing she does is to make a second, larger matter printer by printing the smaller parts and assembling them together. And then there were two.

And why not let's make a third (or fifth) one too.

She can either keep this a secret, print perfectly counterfeited money, pure 24k gold, or something else valuable and live happy; or she can start spreading these printers around under the misguided idea that everyone should have one, thinking that food scarcity and water scarcity and insulin scarcity will become a thing of the past. Maybe since she stopped reading before the end of this article (like many people will do).

And now there is a printer in every house in the neighborhood.

Shortly After the Short-Term

Now that material goods are not scarce, all it takes is some yutz to decide to print a kilogram of U-235, nitroglycerin, or something else that will place me on a government watchlist for mentioning.

It costs a lot of money to start making weapons on a large scale. That is the reason we don't have a proliferation of nuclear weapons for non-governmental actors, since it is very obvious when building a refinery. That is why it is (usually) difficult to get guns since you need a factory to manufacture guns, transport to move them around, and trackable transactions to purchase a gun. However, the matter printer turns this all on its head. A supply chain and manufacturing plant is not needed because you can get whatever you need easily from the matter printer. A small matter printer is also, well, small. It can be hidden in a house, or a basement. It does not need to be a very large facility visible by satellite or by a spy plane.

Solution! Printer DRM!

Because we all know how effective DRM is. Besides, just one matter printer needs to be cracked before everyone has one.

Ok, can we just assume that, by a quirk of the nanomachines powering the printer, the printer can't make explosives or radioactive materials?

This is a piece of fiction, after all. I am sure you can suspend disbelief for a bit.

Economic Collapse

Now, nothing is scarce, everyone has everything material they want, energy needs are fully supplied by the matter-energy converters, and the standard of living is propelled to new heights!

One small issue: our magic matter printer cannot solve everything. Commodities that cannot be created by the matter printer will become extremely valuable, such as real estate.

Real Estate

We already see that those who own the most real estate create inequality (rent hikes, etc). This will only get worse since controlling real estate is one of the few ways to control power in our post-matter-printer world.

Now we have the question, how will rent be paid (assuming that the lack of a barrier to weapons does nothing to spark a revolution)? We still have to follow the laws of physics, even with our magic matter printers.

On the other hand, a matter-energy converter could also reduce the value of real estate. In an economy that is driven more by intellectual output, the workers' location becomes less important (see the recent work-from-home movement). The demand for expensive real estate in cities is heavily reduced since there is less incentive to live in cities.

On the third(?) hand, property prices in cities may not change much. Service-oriented economies will remain service-oriented economies, and human services are best done in areas with other humans—cities. People who go to cities for the lifestyle or for their service-oriented jobs (think doctor) will remain there. A large demand for city housing will remain, whereas demand for outside-of-city housing also will remain (since people may want to move out of the city due to shifting job climates, etc).

I am not an economist, so this is unfortunately a section with a lot of conjecture. Feel free to contact me if you are qualified to make economic opinions and have them.

Matter Collection, Distribution, and Currency

Now that everybody and their dog has a matter printer, they first need to put matter into the printer so that they can print out food, water, medicine, or a new TV. An easy solution that would be pretty easy to use would be water. Many houses are already connected to water lines, and the printers can just take water in and spit out whatever you decide you want that day.

Furthermore, sewage in the house can be recycled into matter. After all, a kilogram of feces has the same energy as a kilogram of anything else.

So, now that trash and waste is recycled, most houses will only need a trickle of matter to replenish what is lost to the environment as well as the comparatively minuscule power needs.

Perhaps now currency would be matter itself. I could send you two hundred grams of imaginary matter (through Apple Pay, naturally) for you to watch my dog over the weekend. Or maybe the backing for the currency would become matter. One US dollar is one kilogram (not unlike the gold standard). Or maybe the currency remains fiat, who knows.

Internet

Unfortunately, you can't print yourself more internet, so I doubt this industry will be disrupted as much. Besides, how are you going to get the printer files for your matter printer other than by going on the internet? It certainly brings up the question about a new economy around printer files. Would people do pay-per-print? Could McDonald's have official McBurgers available for printing for a microtransaction? Then, there's the problem of printer piracy, but people may be willing to sacrifice money for convenience (if the economy shift doesn't change those thought patterns, that is).

Piracy is difficult, and Netflix is easy. The same principle applies to the matter printers. Downloading a .mp3 (Magic Printer 3D) file and sideloading it on a USB drive and doing whatever else is difficult, but one-click microtransactions are easy.

Luxury Goods

My guess is that some companies will continue to manufacture goods that are "brand name"-y, like Apple. Maybe they'll replace their manufacturing with a special matter printer that will print the Apple-branded devices on-demand and put one in every store? Until, eventually, one disgruntled Apple employee decides that they no longer like Apple and leaks the printing file on the internet, and Apple devices are no longer artificially scarce and worth 1099 kilogram-dollars.

But don't discount the belief in "branded" products just yet. I'm sure there are people who would want their items in their house to be "handmade" or "traditionally manufactured" on an authentic assembly line. Because now that is what will remain scarce: handmade luxury goods.

Services

Let's hope that you can't print yourself a new doctor or other human. Since that would cause a lot of issues around say, the ethics of cloning and slavery. So, the service economy will continue running (and will likely be in even higher demand than before as people struggle to show off their wealth and spend their hard-earned matter).

Perhaps a new job will emerge: matter printer programmers and technicians, since printers now tend to break pretty easily. I wonder if that paradigm will continue with our magic matter printers.

So you're saying that the outlook of the economy is looking good?

Not quite. There is likely going to be an increase in the gap between wealth classes. How many people do you know who live in apartments? Who have mortgages? Exceedingly few people are unencumbered by their property, if they have any. Fewer still have zero reliance on services by other humans. The services and luxury goods will remain firmly with the "haves", until somebody invents a robotic doctor.

Von-Neumann Probes

Because matter printers can make more of themselves, some imbecile could decide to create a self-replicating swarm of machines that do nothing except eat matter and make more machines. This is generally considered as a bad idea.

Being More Optimistic

The matter printer will usher in a new age of innovation and likely violence. If society finds a way to be stabilized, well, it only takes one moron to decide that he wants to live on another planet and print a rocket.

There are still a lot of what-ifs. If you do decide to invent one of these matter printers or perhaps steal one, be sure you can figure out whether you would like to be seen as the savior of humanity or as the person who gave every terrorist a nuclear weapon.

AN: This is a deviation from what I usually write. I had been thinking about this for a while, but last night was when it really clicked in my head that magic matter printers would be pretty bad.

This post is also a LOT of conjecture. So, hopefully that's fine with you all.

Also, sorry for not having a post in a while. I was out on vacation. I don't know when the next one will be out since I'm moving into college in like four days (super excited)

As always, if there is anything that I missed or that I have wrong, feel free to contact me.

Thanks to Nick Zana for proofreading and contributing.

The bad art was done by me, and the good stuff was made by Zach Weinersmith from SMBC
